quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because all 3 chords ina ii V | are in the parent key, so Dm7-G7-Cmaj7 are all in C
major, you can use the tonic major scale to solo over all of those chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the foundational harmonic rule of the chapter — it justifies using a single scale over all three chords of the ii V I progression, which is the core soloing concept being taught.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Start by learning these scale shapes one at a time, add them to your soloing
workout, then combine them to cover the fretboard in your solos.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives the explicit method for progressing through the material: individual shapes first, then integration across the fretboard — a structured practice prescription.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Start by memorizing the scale shape, then working it with a metronome.

As soon as you’re ready, you know the scale but it’s not perfect, throw on the
backing track and solo with this scale over the C major ii V I.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This defines the two-phase practice protocol — metronome drilling followed by applied improvisation over the backing track — which is the method's characteristic move for internalizing each shape.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
here’s the 6*-string C major scale to memorize and add to your solos
over the backing track.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies Shape 1 as rooted on the 6th string, establishing the positional anchor for the first scale fingering.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Here’s the second major scale shape from the 5" string to learn, memorize, work
with a metronome and solo with over the backing tracks when ready.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies Shape 2 as rooted on the 5th string, establishing the positional anchor for the second scale fingering and completing the two-shape system taught in the chapter.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
From there, solo over the backing track with this scale shape, then with the 6-
string shape only, then mix both together.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the integration instruction that teaches students to eventually combine both shapes across the neck, which is the culminating skill of the chapter.
